topics.title: Articles
topics.description: Learn German articles: definite (der, die, das) and indefinite (ein, eine, einen, etc.) articles, including their gender and case forms.

lessons.title: Definite Articles
lessons.explanation: German has three definite articles: der (masculine), die (feminine), das (neuter). They show the gender and case of the noun. In the nominative case (subject), they are: der Mann, die Frau, das Kind. The article always agrees with the noun's gender — this is fixed for each noun and must be learned together with the word.

rules.text: Masculine nouns take 'der' in the nominative case (subject position).
Feminine nouns take 'die' in the nominative case.
Neuter nouns take 'das' in the nominative case.
In the accusative case (direct object), masculine changes der → den; feminine and neuter stay the same.

examples.english: The child plays in the garden

exceptions.german: Gender is not based on biology
exceptions.english: das Mädchen (the girl) is neuter because of the -chen suffix, even though a girl is female
das Häuschen, das Fräulein — words ending in -chen or -lein are always das

importantNotes: Memorize every noun together with its article (der Apfel, die Banane).
The article tells you the gender — do not guess from meaning.
Plural of all genders uses 'die': die Männer, die Frauen, die Kinder.

commonMistakes.english: Should be: der Mann — Mann is masculine
Should be: die Frau — Frau is feminine

lessons.title: Indefinite Articles
lessons.explanation: Indefinite articles (ein, eine, einen, einem, einer) mean 'a' or 'an'. They are used for singular countable nouns when we do not specify which one. Like definite articles, they change by gender and case: ein (masc. nom/acc), eine (fem. nom/acc), ein (neut. nom/acc), einem (masc/neut. dat), einer (fem. dat).

rules.text: Masculine singular takes 'ein' in nominative and accusative.
Feminine singular takes 'eine' in nominative and accusative.
Neuter singular takes 'ein' in nominative and accusative.

exceptions.english: We use 'kein' (no) or zero article: Ich habe Bücher (I have books)

importantNotes: After negation 'kein' follows the same pattern as 'ein'.
Indefinite articles never appear in the plural.

commonMistakes.english: Should be: ein Mann — Mann is masculine
Should be: eine Frau — Frau is feminine

topics.title: Personal Pronouns
topics.description: Learn German personal pronouns in nominative: ich, du, er, sie, es, wir, ihr, sie, Sie — and how they change by case.

lessons.title: Subject Pronouns (Nominative)
lessons.explanation: Personal pronouns replace nouns in sentences. German has formal and informal forms. They are ALWAYS capitalized in German (Ich, Du, Er...). The nominative case is used when the pronoun is the subject (who does the action).

rules.text: ich (I) — first person singular
du (you) — informal singular, used with friends, family, children
er / sie / es (he / she / it) — third person singular, matching gender of the noun

examples.german: Er arbeitet im Büro (he = der Mann)

rules.text: wir (we) — first person plural
ihr (you) — informal plural, used with a group of friends/family
sie (they) / Sie (you, formal) — third person plural OR formal singular/plural

examples.german: Ich lese, du schaust, er schläft
examples.english: I read, you watch, he sleeps

examples.german: Wir gehen, ihr kommt, sie bleiben
examples.english: We go, you come, they stay

exceptions.english: All personal pronouns are capitalized in German: Ich, Du, Er, Sie...

importantNotes: The formal 'Sie' looks identical to plural 'sie' but is ALWAYS capitalized and uses plural verb forms.
Use 'du' with one person you know well; use 'Sie' with strangers, elders, in business.
There is no separate word for 'it' by person — 'es' is used for neuter nouns and things.

commonMistakes.english: Should be capitalized when formal: Sie

lessons.title: Object Pronouns (Accusative & Dative)
lessons.explanation: When pronouns are NOT the subject, they change form by case. Accusative (direct object) and dative (indirect object) forms are essential at A1.

rules.text: Accusative pronouns: mich, dich, ihn, sie, es, uns, euch, sie, Sie
Dative pronouns: mir, dir, ihm, ihr, ihm, uns, euch, ihnen, Ihnen

examples.german: Er gibt mir das Buch (He gives me the book)

exceptions.german: es stays 'es' in both accusative and dative
exceptions.english: Ich sehe es / Er gibt es mir

importantNotes: Dative answers 'wem?' (to/for whom).
Accusative answers 'wen?' (whom).

commonMistakes.english: Should be: Er gibt mir das Buch (dative)

topics.title: Verb Conjugation
topics.description: Learn how to conjugate regular and irregular verbs in the present tense, including the key verbs sein, haben, werden.

lessons.title: Present Tense — Regular Verbs
lessons.explanation: Regular verbs (weak verbs) follow a predictable pattern. Remove the infinitive ending -en to get the stem, then add personal endings. The stem never changes. Example: machen → mach- + e/st/t/en/t/en.

rules.text: Remove -en to get the stem: machen → mach-
Add endings: -e, -st, -t, -en, -t, -en (ich, du, er/sie/es, wir, ihr, sie/Sie)
Verbs ending in -s, -ß, -z add only -t for du (no extra -st): heißen → du heißt

importantNotes: The conjugated verb is always in POSITION 2 of a main clause.
Learn the stem and apply the same endings to all regular verbs.

lessons.title: Present Tense — Irregular Verbs
lessons.explanation: Irregular verbs (strong and mixed) change their stem vowel in the present tense for du and er/sie/es. The endings are the same as regular verbs. The most important ones to learn first are sein (to be), haben (to have), and werden (to become).

rules.text: Strong verbs change the vowel: sehen → ich sehe, du siehst, er sieht

examples.german: sehen: ich sehe, du siehst, er sieht
examples.english: see: I see, you see, he sees

examples.german: geben: ich gebe, du gibst, er gibt
examples.english: give: I give, you give, he gives

examples.german: lesen: ich lese, du liest, er liest
examples.english: read: I read, you read, he reads

rules.text: Mixed verbs keep stem change only in du/er: finden → ich finde, du findest, er findet

examples.german: finden: ich finde, du findest, er findet
examples.english: find: I find, you find, he finds

examples.german: kennen: ich kenne, du kennst, er kennt
examples.english: know: I know, you know, he knows

rules.text: sein (to be) is completely irregular: ich bin, du bist, er/sie/es ist, wir sind, ihr seid, sie/Sie sind
haben (to have): ich habe, du hast, er/sie/es hat, wir haben, ihr habt, sie/Sie haben

exceptions.german: sein, haben, werden — the three key auxiliaries
exceptions.english: Must be memorized separately; they appear in almost every sentence

importantNotes: Learn sein and haben FIRST — they are used everywhere.
Only du and er/sie/es get the vowel change in strong verbs; ich, wir, ihr stay normal.

topics.title: Present Tense
topics.description: Master the present tense (Präsens) for describing current actions, habits, facts, and scheduled future events.

lessons.title: Present Tense Usage
lessons.explanation: The German present tense (Präsens) is very versatile. It describes actions happening now, daily routines, general facts, and — unlike English — also scheduled future events when a time word is present.

rules.text: Used for actions happening now (with 'gerade' or 'jetzt')

examples.english: He is working in the garden

rules.text: Used for daily routines and habits (with time expressions like 'jeden Tag')

examples.german: Ich gehe jeden Tag zur Arbeit
examples.english: I go to work every day

rules.text: Used for scheduled future events (present + future time word)

exceptions.english: For emphasis on the future, use ich werde ...: Ich werde morgen kommen (I will come tomorrow)

importantNotes: German present covers English present simple AND present continuous.
For ongoing actions, add 'gerade' (right now) — there is no separate -ing form.

commonMistakes.english: Better with gerade: Ich lerne gerade Deutsch

topics.title: Modal Verbs
topics.description: Learn modal verbs: können, müssen, wollen, sollen, mögen/möchte, dürfen — and the two-verb sentence structure.

lessons.title: Basic Modal Verbs
lessons.explanation: Modal verbs express ability, permission, obligation, desire, or preference. They have a special conjugation (no -t in er/sie/es) and they 'push' the main verb to the END of the sentence in its infinitive form. Structure: subject + modal (conjugated) + ... + main verb (infinitive).

rules.text: können (can / be able to): ich kann, du kannst, er kann, wir können, ihr könnt, sie können
müssen (must / have to): ich muss, du musst, er muss...
wollen (want to): ich will, du willst, er will...
möchte (would like — polite form of mögen): ich möchte, du möchtest, er möchte...

examples.english: What would you like to eat?

rules.text: dürfen (may / be allowed to): ich darf, du darfst, er darf...

examples.english: I am not allowed to smoke

exceptions.english: The main verb stays in infinitive at the END: Ich möchte gehen (NOT ich gehe möchte)

importantNotes: Modal verbs have NO -t ending for er/sie/es (er kann, not er kannt).
Use 'möchte' for polite requests instead of 'will'.

commonMistakes.german: Ich möchte gehen (correct!) but ich will nicht gehen (correct)
commonMistakes.english: Do not say 'ich gehe möchte' — verb goes to the end

topics.title: Negation
topics.description: Learn how to form negative sentences: 'nicht' (negates verbs/adjectives) and 'kein' (negates nouns).

lessons.title: Nicht vs Kein
lessons.explanation: German has two main negations. 'nicht' negates verbs, adjectives, and adverbs. 'kein' negates nouns that would otherwise take an indefinite article (a/an). 'kein' declines like 'ein' (kein, keine, kein, keinen, keinem, keiner).

rules.text: nicht — negates verbs, adjectives, adverbs, whole sentences
kein — negates a noun when 'a/an' would be used; declines by gender/case
Position of 'nicht': usually before the word it negates; at the end when negating the whole verb

examples.german: Ich komme nicht morgen, sondern heute
examples.english: I'm not coming tomorrow but today

examples.german: Das ist kein Hund, das ist eine Katze
examples.english: That is not a dog, that is a cat

exceptions.english: Use 'nicht' with definite articles: Ich sehe den Mann nicht (not keinem)

importantNotes: kein = 'not a / not any'.
nicht position varies: before adjective/verb-part, at end for the whole sentence.

commonMistakes.english: Should be: Ich habe kein Buch

commonMistakes.german: Ich sehe nicht den Mann (wrong word order)
commonMistakes.english: Better: Ich sehe den Mann nicht

topics.title: Questions
topics.description: Learn to form questions: W-questions (was, wer, wann...) and yes/no questions (verb-first).

lessons.title: W-Questions
lessons.explanation: W-questions use question words (interrogatives). The question word stands in position 1, the conjugated verb in position 2, and the rest follows. Common W-words: was (what), wer (who), wann (when), warum (why), wie (how), wo (where), wohin (where to), woher (where from).

rules.text: Question word + verb (position 2) + rest
Common W-words and their meanings

examples.german: Wie heißt du? (how are you called)

importantNotes: The verb NEVER leaves position 2 in W-questions.
Wo = where (static), wohin = where to (movement).

lessons.title: Yes/No Questions
lessons.explanation: For yes/no questions (no question word), the conjugated verb moves to position 1, before the subject. This is called 'inversion'. Answer with 'Ja' (yes) or 'Nein' (no), often repeating the verb.

rules.text: Verb in position 1, then subject, then rest
Short answers: Ja, ... / Nein, ...

exceptions.english: Möchtest du einen Kaffee? (Would you like a coffee?)

importantNotes: Yes/no questions always start with the conjugated verb.
The subject comes immediately after the verb.

commonMistakes.german: Du kommst aus Berlin? (word order wrong for a question)
commonMistakes.english: Should be: Kommst du aus Berlin?

topics.title: Word Order
topics.description: Learn German sentence structure: verb position, time-manner-place, and main vs subordinate clauses.

lessons.title: Basic Word Order
lessons.explanation: In main clauses the conjugated verb is ALWAYS in position 2. The first position can be any element (subject, object, time). The typical order after the verb is: Subject — (not) — Time — Manner — Place — rest. With modal verbs, the main verb goes to the end.

rules.text: Verb in position 2; first position flexible

examples.german: Ich gehe heute Abend ins Kino
examples.english: I go to the cinema tonight

examples.german: Heute Abend gehe ich ins Kino
examples.english: Tonight I go to the cinema

rules.text: Time — Manner — Place order at the end

examples.german: Er arbeitet morgen früh (time) im Büro (place)
examples.english: He works tomorrow morning in the office

examples.german: Wir fahren mit dem Bus (manner) nach Hause (place)

rules.text: With a modal verb, the infinitive goes to the END

examples.german: Ich will morgen nach Berlin fahren
examples.english: I want to drive to Berlin tomorrow

exceptions.english: In yes/no questions the verb is in position 1; in commands it is also first.

importantNotes: The verb is the anchor — count to it for position 2.
In subordinate clauses (weil, dass, wenn) the verb goes to the END.

commonMistakes.english: Better: Ich gehe heute ins Kino (time before place)
Better: Ich will nach Berlin fahren (infinitive at end)

topics.title: Cases
topics.description: Learn German cases: nominative (subject) and accusative (direct object) — the two most important at A1.

lessons.title: Nominative and Accusative
lessons.explanation: German has four cases. At A1 you need mainly Nominative (subject — who does it?) and Accusative (direct object — whom/what does it affect?). Articles and pronouns change to show the case.

rules.text: Nominative = subject (wer? was?): der, die, das / ein, eine, ein

examples.german: Der Mann liest (wer liest? → der Mann)
Die Frau arbeitet (was arbeitet? → die Frau)

rules.text: Accusative = direct object (wen? was?): den, die, das / einen, eine, ein

examples.german: Ich sehe den Mann (wen sehe ich? → den Mann)
Er liebt die Musik (was liebt er? → die Musik)

rules.text: Accusative pronouns: mich, dich, ihn, sie, es, uns, euch, Sie

examples.german: Ich rufe ihn an (I call him)

exceptions.english: der → den: Ich kaufe den Tisch (not das Tisch)

importantNotes: Feminine and neuter articles do NOT change in accusative.
Only masculine singular changes: der → den, ein → einen.

commonMistakes.english: Should be: Ich sehe den Mann

commonMistakes.german: Ich habe eine Frau gesehen (correct — feminine stays)

topics.title: Prepositions
topics.description: Learn common German prepositions and the cases they take: accusative, dative, and two-way prepositions.

lessons.title: Basic Prepositions & Cases
lessons.explanation: Prepositions show relationships (place, time, manner). Each preposition demands a specific case. A1 prepositions are grouped by the case they take: accusative, dative, or two-way (which change by movement vs location).

rules.text: Accusative prepositions: für, um, durch, gegen, ohne, bis
Dative prepositions: mit, bei, nach, seit, von, zu, aus, außer

examples.german: Ich fahre mit dem Bus (mit + dative)
Er ist bei der Arbeit (bei + dative)

rules.text: Two-way prepositions (in, an, auf, über, unter, neben, vor, hinter, zwischen): accusative = movement, dative = location

examples.german: Ich gehe in die Schule (movement → accusative)
Ich bin in der Schule (location → dative)
Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch (location → dative)
examples.english: The book lies on the table

examples.german: Wir treffen uns vor dem Kino
examples.english: We meet in front of the cinema

exceptions.english: an + dative for days: am Montag (an dem Montag)

importantNotes: Memorize prepositions together with their case.
Two-way: ask 'wohin?' (where to) → accusative; 'wo?' (where) → dative.

commonMistakes.english: Should be: Ich wohne in Berlin

commonMistakes.german: Ich gehe in dem Garten (wrong for movement)
commonMistakes.english: Should be: Ich gehe in den Garten (accusative, movement)

topics.title: Possessive Pronouns
topics.description: Learn possessive pronouns: mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer, ihr, Ihr — and how they decline.

lessons.title: Possessive Basics
lessons.explanation: Possessive pronouns show ownership. They decline like indefinite articles (ein/eine/ein). The form depends on the OWNER (mein = my, dein = your, etc.) and the gender/case of the owned noun. Forms: mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer, ihr, Ihr.

rules.text: Masculine/Neuter nominative: mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer, ihr, Ihr

examples.german: mein Mann, dein Tisch, sein Kind
examples.english: my husband, your table, his child

rules.text: Feminine/Plural nominative: meine, deine, seine, ihre, unsere, eure, ihre, Ihre

examples.german: meine Frau, deine Katze, ihre Zeit
examples.english: my wife, your cat, her time

rules.text: Accusative masculine: meinen, deinen, seinen... (like einen)

exceptions.english: ihr = 'her' (singular) OR 'their' (plural); Ihr (capital) = 'your' formal

importantNotes: Possessives decline exactly like 'ein'.
euer often drops the 'e' in speech: eur- (eure, euren).

commonMistakes.english: Should be: ein großer Mann (adjective ending with possessive)
Should be: ich sehe meinen Vater (accusative)

topics.title: Numbers
topics.description: Learn German cardinal numbers 0-100, how to build compound numbers, and use them for age, prices, and phone numbers.

lessons.title: Cardinal Numbers
lessons.explanation: German numbers follow specific patterns. Learn 0-100 for A1. Important: German builds compound numbers BACKWARDS — the ones digit comes first, then the tens (einundzwanzig = one-and-twenty = 21).

rules.text: 0-12: null, eins, zwei, drei, vier, fünf, sechs, sieben, acht, neun, zehn, elf, zwölf
Tens: zwanzig, dreißig, vierzig, fünfzig, sechzig, siebzig, achtzig, neunzig
100 = hundert; note 'eins' becomes 'ein' in compounds (einhundert)

exceptions.english: The 'eins' loses the 's' in compounds

importantNotes: Numbers are essential for prices, time, dates, and phone numbers.
Say the ones digit first in numbers above 20.

topics.title: Time
topics.description: Learn to tell time in German, use the 24-hour clock, and talk about days and schedules.

lessons.title: Telling Time
lessons.explanation: To tell time use 'Es ist' + the hour (+ minutes). Germans often use the 24-hour clock in formal contexts. 'halb' means half past the PREVIOUS hour (halb drei = half past two = 2:30).

rules.text: Full hours: Es ist + number + Uhr (e.g., Es ist drei Uhr = 3:00)
Minutes past: NACH (e.g., zehn nach drei = 3:10); Minutes to: VOR (e.g., fünf vor zwölf = 11:55)
halb + next hour = half past previous (halb drei = 2:30)

examples.german: Es ist Viertel nach drei (quarter past)
Ich komme um drei Uhr an

exceptions.english: Viertel nach drei = 3:15; Viertel vor vier = 3:45

importantNotes: Formal contexts use 24-hour format (18 Uhr = 6 PM).
'Morgen' can mean 'morning' or 'tomorrow' — context decides.

commonMistakes.german: Es ist drei Uhr morgen (wrong word order)
commonMistakes.english: Should be: Ich komme morgen um drei Uhr

topics.title: Adjectives
topics.description: Learn German adjectives: basic forms, endings after der/ein words, and comparison (Komparativ).

lessons.title: Adjective Endings (Basic)
lessons.explanation: Adjectives describe nouns. In A1 you mostly use two situations: (1) after a definite article (der/die/das) — the adjective takes a weak ending (-e, -en, -e, -en...); (2) after a possessive or indefinite article (ein/eine/mein) — endings are like 'ein' (-er, -e, -es); (3) after a linking verb with NO article — no ending (Er ist groß).

rules.text: After definite article (der/die/das): adjective gets -e (nom) / -en (acc/plural)

examples.german: Ich sehe den großen Mann (masc acc)

rules.text: After indefinite/possessive (ein/mein): ending like 'ein'
After a linking verb (sein/werden/bleiben), no article: NO ending

importantNotes: A1 uses simple forms; full adjective declension comes later.
Adjective comes BEFORE the noun.

lessons.title: Comparison (Komparativ & Superlativ)
lessons.explanation: Adjectives can be compared. The comparative adds '-er' (or umlaut for some short adjectives); the superlative adds '-st' (or '-est' after s/t/z). Structure: als = 'than'.

rules.text: Comparative = adjective + -er (klein → kleiner)

examples.german: Mein Bruder ist größer als ich
examples.english: My brother is bigger than me

rules.text: Umlaut change for short adjectives: groß→größer, alt→älter, jung→jünger, lang→länger
Superlative = -(e)st (am ...-sten) — 'am' + adjective + -sten

examples.german: Das ist am besten (gut → am besten)
Der Kaffee ist heißer als das Wasser
examples.english: The coffee is hotter than the water

exceptions.english: gut → besser, viel → mehr, gern → lieber

importantNotes: Use 'als' for 'than' (never 'wie' in comparison).
gut → besser, viel → mehr are irregular — memorize them.

topics.title: Separable Verbs
topics.description: Learn separable-prefix verbs ( trennbare Verben) and how the prefix moves to the end in main clauses.

lessons.title: Verb Separation
lessons.explanation: Many German verbs have a prefix (an-, auf-, aus-, ein-, mit-, nach-, vor-, zu-, ab-, weg-) that separates from the verb in present tense main clauses. The prefix moves to the VERY END of the sentence. In questions/commands, the separated prefix joins the stem.

rules.text: Main clause: verb in position 2, prefix at the END
Question/command: prefix stays joined to the verb
With a modal verb, the infinitive (with prefix) stays at the end

examples.english: I want to get up early
We must turn the light on

examples.german: Wir treffen uns später (treffen + uns = reflexive)

exceptions.english: be-, ge-, er-, ver-, zer- NEVER separate (e.g., verstehen, bekommen)

importantNotes: The prefix is stressed in speech: AUFstehen.
Do NOT separate in infinitive or with modal verbs.

commonMistakes.english: Should be: Ich mache das Fenster zu

topics.title: Plural Forms
topics.description: Learn how German nouns form the plural and why the article becomes 'die' for all plurals.

lessons.title: Forming Plurals
lessons.explanation: German plurals are formed in five main ways: -(e)n, -e, -er, no ending (with umlaut), or -s (for foreign words). The definite article for ALL plurals is 'die'. There is no separate 'they' article by gender — everything plural is 'die'.

rules.text: Plural with -(e)n: most feminine nouns and many masculines (die Frau → die Frauen)
Plural with -e (often with umlaut): many masculine and neuter (der Mann → die Männer)
Plural with -er (with umlaut) for many neuter and some masculine (das Kind → die Kinder)
Plural with -s: mostly foreign words and abbreviations (das Auto → die Autos)

exceptions.english: Plural form must often be memorized with the noun; dictionaries show it

importantNotes: All plural articles are 'die' (nom/acc) and 'den' (dat).
Learn the plural form together with each noun.

commonMistakes.english: Plural article is always die/den, never der

topics.title: Dative Case
topics.description: Learn the dative case (indirect object, wem?) and dative prepositions at A1.

lessons.title: Dative Basics
lessons.explanation: The dative case shows the indirect object (to/for whom?). It answers 'wem?'. Articles: dem (masc), der (fem), dem (neut), den (plural). Dative pronouns: mir, dir, ihm, ihr, ihm, uns, euch, ihnen, Ihnen.

rules.text: Dative articles: dem, der, dem, den

examples.german: Ich gebe dem Mann das Buch (wem? → dem Mann)
examples.english: I give the man the book

examples.german: Er hilft der Frau (wem? → der Frau)

rules.text: Dative pronouns: mir, dir, ihm, ihr, uns, euch, ihnen
Dative prepositions: mit, bei, nach, seit, von, zu, aus, außer (always dative)

examples.german: Wir gehen zu dem Freund (zum Freund)
Das Geschenk ist für meine Mutter
examples.english: The gift is for my mother

exceptions.english: zu + dem = zum; in + dem = im; an + dem = am

importantNotes: Dative usually comes BEFORE accusative: Ich gebe dem Mann (dat) den Apfel (acc).
Many verbs require dative: helfen, danken, gefallen, antworten.

commonMistakes.english: Should be: mit dem Mann (masc dative)

topics.title: Imperative
topics.description: Learn commands in German: du, wir, and Sie forms.

lessons.title: Imperative Forms
lessons.explanation: The imperative gives commands/requests. German has three forms at A1: du (informal singular), wir (let's), and Sie (formal). The verb is in position 1. For 'du', drop the -st and often add -e (or nothing for regular verbs).

rules.text: du-imperative: stem (+e optional), NO pronoun, capital first letter
wir-imperative: same as 'wir' present, means 'let's'
Sie-imperative: infinitive + Sie (formal, polite)

exceptions.english: du: sei / wir: seien / Sie: seien Sie

importantNotes: For 'du', strong verbs with vowel change (e→i) keep it: gib!, iss!, lies!.
Add 'bitte' for politeness.

commonMistakes.english: Should be: Komm! (no pronoun in du-imperative)

topics.title: Es gibt
topics.description: Learn 'es gibt' (there is / there are) and the difference from 'da sein' (to be there).

lessons.title: Es gibt (There is / There are)
lessons.explanation: 'es gibt' means 'there is / there are' and is used to state existence of something. IMPORTANT: the noun after 'es gibt' is ALWAYS in the accusative case (singular or plural). It is different from 'da sein' (to be located there).

rules.text: 'es gibt' + accusative noun (singular or plural)
Negative: es gibt + kein/keine
'da sein' (to be there) vs 'es gibt' (existence): Wo ist...? / Das ist da

examples.german: Das Buch ist da (the book is there)
Was gibt es zum Essen? (what is there to eat?)
In Berlin gibt es viele Parks
examples.english: In Berlin there are many parks

exceptions.english: Even plural: Es gibt die Bücher (accusative, not den)

importantNotes: 'es gibt' NEVER changes — it is always 'es gibt' regardless of singular/plural.
Use 'es gibt' for existence; use 'ist/sind' for location.

commonMistakes.english: Should be: Es gibt einen Apfel (accusative)

commonMistakes.german: Es gibt die Bücher (correct — plural accusative)

topics.title: Demonstrative Pronouns
topics.description: Learn demonstrative pronouns dieser/diese/dieses (this/these) to point out specific things.

lessons.title: Dieser / Diese / Dieses
lessons.explanation: Demonstrative pronouns point to specific nouns ('this' / 'that' / 'these' / 'those'). They decline like definite articles but start with 'dies-'. Forms: dieser (m), diese (f), dieses (n), diese (pl).

rules.text: Singular: dieser (m), diese (f), dieses (n)
Plural: diese (these/those)
Short form 'dies' (this one) used alone

examples.german: Was ist das? — Das ist dies hier
examples.english: What is that? — This is this one here

exceptions.english: 'dies' points closer; 'das' can mean 'that' but also 'the'

importantNotes: Decline 'dies-' exactly like 'der/die/das'.
Use to emphasize or distinguish one item.

topics.title: Conjunctions
topics.description: Learn coordinating conjunctions (und, aber, oder, denn, sondern) that keep verb in position 2.

lessons.title: Coordinating Conjunctions
lessons.explanation: Coordinating conjunctions join two main clauses of equal weight. They do NOT change word order — each clause keeps its verb in position 2. Key ones: und (and), aber (but), oder (or), denn (because), sondern (but rather — after a negation).

rules.text: und (and) / oder (or) — join clauses, verb stays position 2 in both

examples.german: Ich lerne Deutsch und er lernt Englisch
examples.english: I learn German and he learns English
Do you drink coffee or tea?

rules.text: aber (but) / denn (because) — same word order

examples.german: Er ist müde, aber er arbeitet
examples.english: He is tired, but he works

examples.german: Ich bleibe zu Hause, denn es regnet
examples.english: I stay home because it rains

rules.text: sondern (but rather) — used ONLY after a negation, replaces 'aber'

examples.german: Er ist nicht krank, sondern müde
examples.english: He is not sick but (rather) tired

examples.german: Das ist kein Hund, sondern eine Katze
examples.english: That is not a dog but a cat

examples.german: Ich mag Kaffee, aber mein Bruder mag Tee
examples.english: I like coffee but my brother likes tea

examples.german: Wir gehen ins Kino, denn wir haben Zeit
examples.english: We go to the cinema because we have time

exceptions.english: Use sondern only after a negated clause; otherwise use aber

importantNotes: Coordinating conjunctions do NOT push the verb to the end (that is subordinating conjunctions like weil).
Both clauses are independent.

commonMistakes.german: Er ist nicht krank, aber müde (slightly off)
commonMistakes.english: Better: Er ist nicht krank, sondern müde
weil is subordinating — verb goes to end; use denn for 'because' with normal order

topics.title: Impersonal 'man'
topics.description: Learn the impersonal pronoun 'man' (one / you / they) used for general statements.

lessons.title: Using 'man'
lessons.explanation: 'man' is an impersonal pronoun meaning 'one', 'you' (general), or 'they' (people in general). It is only used in the nominative singular and conjugates like 'er' (man macht, man sagt). It avoids saying a specific person.

rules.text: man = 'one / you (general) / people' — conjugates like er/sie/es

examples.english: One says / People say German is hard

rules.text: In accusative/dative, 'man' becomes 'einen' / 'einem' (neutral indefinite)

examples.german: Wie sagt man das auf Deutsch?
examples.english: How does one say that in German?

exceptions.english: 'man' is always lowercase and impersonal

importantNotes: Use 'man' to make general statements without naming who.
It always takes 3rd person singular verb forms.

commonMistakes.english: Should be: Man ist (3rd person singular)

topics.title: Days & Months
topics.description: Learn the days of the week, months, seasons, and how to say dates in German.

lessons.title: Days, Months, Seasons
lessons.explanation: Days of the week and months are NOT capitalized in German (except at sentence start). Seasons: der Frühling, der Sommer, der Herbst, der Winter. Dates use ordinal numbers: der erste Mai (the first of May).

rules.text: Days: montag, dienstag, mittwoch, donnerstag, freitag, samstag/sonnabend, sonntag
Months: januar, februar, märz, april, mai, juni, juli, august, september, oktober, november, dezember
Seasons: der Frühling, der Sommer, der Herbst, der Winter (all masculine)

examples.german: Wir fahren im Juli in Urlaub
examples.english: We go on vacation in July

exceptions.english: Both mean Saturday; sonnabend is regional (north)

importantNotes: Use 'am' + day (am Montag), 'im' + month/season (im Juli).
Days/months are lowercase in German.

topics.title: Countries & Nationalities
topics.description: Learn to name countries, say where you come from, and form nationalities/adjectives of origin.

lessons.title: Countries & Nationalities
lessons.explanation: To say where you are from use 'Ich komme aus + [country] (dative)'. Nationalities are adjectives and decline like normal adjectives. Most country names are neuter (das) or feminine (die), a few masculine (der).

rules.text: Ich komme aus + dative country name
Nationality adjectives (decline like adjectives): deutsch, türkisch, spanisch, russisch, amerikanisch
Languages: Deutsch, Englisch, Spanisch, Französisch, Russisch (capitalized nouns)

examples.german: Woher kommst du? — Ich komme aus Spanien
examples.english: Where are you from? — I'm from Spain

exceptions.english: die Schweiz, die Türkei, die USA, der Iran, das Vereinigte Königreich

importantNotes: Nationalities are adjectives (lowercase, declining) unless used as nouns (der Deutsche).
Languages are always capitalized nouns in German.

commonMistakes.english: Should be: ich spreche Deutsch (language capitalized)

topics.title: Reflexive Verbs
topics.description: Learn basic reflexive verbs with 'sich' (myself, yourself...) at A1.

lessons.title: Reflexive Verbs (Basic)
lessons.explanation: Reflexive verbs have a pronoun that refers back to the subject (myself, yourself). The reflexive pronoun declines: mich (acc), dich, sich, uns, euch, sich. Common A1 reflexives: sich freuen (to be happy), sich waschen (to wash), sich interessieren für (to be interested in).

rules.text: Reflexive pronouns in accusative: mich, dich, sich, uns, euch, sich
sich interessieren für + accusative (to be interested in)

examples.german: Freust du dich auf die Reise?
examples.english: Are you looking forward to the trip?

exceptions.english: sie freuen sich (they are happy)

importantNotes: Most reflexive pronouns are accusative; some are dative (sich dativ).
'sich' is the same for er/sie/es and sie/Sie.

commonMistakes.german: Ich freue mich (correct) but er freut mir (wrong)

commonMistakes.english: NOT wir waschen uns selbst (uns is enough)

topics.title: Weather
topics.description: Learn to describe the weather and common weather expressions in German.

lessons.title: Weather Expressions
lessons.explanation: Weather is described with 'es' (it) + verb or adjective. Common verbs: regnen (to rain), schneien (to snow), sonnen (die Sonne scheint). Adjectives: warm, kalt, heiß, küh, sonnig, windig, neblig.

rules.text: es + verb: regnen, schneien, (die Sonne) scheinen
es + adjective (with sein): warm, kalt, heiß, sonnig, windig
Common phrases: Wie ist das Wetter? / Das Wetter ist schön

examples.english: What is the weather like today?

examples.german: Heute ist es kühl und windig
examples.english: Today it is cool and windy

exceptions.english: The sun shines — use die Sonne

importantNotes: Weather sentences use 'es' as a dummy subject.
Adjectives after 'ist' take NO ending (es ist kalt).

commonMistakes.english: Should be: es ist kalt (no ending after sein)

topics.title: Common Verbs
topics.description: Essential high-frequency verbs for A1 communication, beyond haben and sein.

lessons.title: Essential A1 Verbs
lessons.explanation: These high-frequency verbs appear in almost every A1 conversation. Learn their meaning and basic forms: gehen (go), kommen (come), machen (do/make), essen (eat), trinken (drink), schlafen (sleep), sprechen (speak), verstehen (understand), kaufen (buy), finden (find/like), gefallen (please), wissen (know a fact).

rules.text: Daily activity verbs: gehen, kommen, machen, essen, trinken, schlafen
Communication verbs: sprechen, verstehen, wissen, sagen
Preference verbs: gefallen (to please, dative!), finden (to find/think), mögen (to like)

examples.english: I like the book (lit. the book pleases me)
I find the film good (I like it)
What do you do for a living?
How do you like the city?

exceptions.english: Das gefällt mir (NOT ich) — the thing is subject, person is dative

importantNotes: gefallen = 'to please' — the person is in dative (mir, dir).
finden + adjective = opinion ('I find it good' = I like it).

commonMistakes.english: Should be: Das Buch gefällt mir

commonMistakes.german: ich weiß (correct) vs ich kenne (know a person/thing)
